task.title: Validate a Markdown file from the command line
task.shortdesc: Run md-validate validate against a single Markdown file to check it against your team's rule set. The output shows which rules passed, which failed, and what you need to change.

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Run markdown-validator against a single Markdown file and review the pass/fail report to identify and fix validation failures.

ul.li: markdown-validator is installed and the virtual environment is active. See .
You have the path to your team's rule set file. If you are unsure, ask your content lead. The rule set is typically stored in the documentation repository under a folder named rules/.

context.p: Validating your article locally before opening a pull request lets you catch and fix problems before the CI pipeline sees them. Fix all Required failures before you push; you can choose whether to address Suggested failures.

step.cmd: Open a terminal and navigate to your documentation repository.
Run the validate command with the path to your file and your team's rule set.

info.p: Replace docs/my-article.md with the path to your file and rules/tutorial.json with the path to your team's rule set.

step.stepresult: The tool prints a validation report to the terminal.

step.cmd: Review the output and identify any failed rules.

info.p: A passing report looks like this:
A report with failures looks like this:
info.codeblock:
✓ [1] topic-must-be-tutorial ✗ [2] must-have-h1 → The document must have exactly one H1 heading. ✓ [3] description-must-exist
Score: 2/3 FAILED
info.p: Rules marked with ✗ failed. The arrow (→) shows the mitigation message — a plain-English description of what to fix.

step.cmd: Fix the failures identified in the report.

info.p: Open your Markdown file in your editor and address each failing rule using the mitigation message as your guide. For detailed guidance on interpreting and acting on a report, see Read the validation report.

step.cmd: Re-run the validate command to confirm all failures are resolved.
step.stepresult: The score and PASSED/FAILED status reflect your changes.

result.p: Your file either passes all Required rules (score shows PASSED) or shows the remaining failures to fix.

ul.li: To understand what each part of the report means and how to fix specific failure types, see Read the validation report.
To validate an entire folder of files at once, see Validate a directory.
